config_status.note: The current Chapter 5 main path uses experiments/configs/openworkload_v1_local_realworld_lora8.json. Keep this file only as a small reference config.

method_definition.phase1: Use chunk-size control to return from long prefills to the scheduler boundary earlier.
method_definition.phase2: Use execution escape and scheduler-side priority promotion to reshape the next scheduled window.
method_definition.adaptation: The current main config uses workload-pressure adaptation; this legacy reference config does not enable that policy.

real_world_scenario.name: V1 Open-Workload Mixed Arrival Regression
real_world_scenario.description: This suite re-runs Wave-Slice on the V1 engine using locally available open-weight models and open datasets. Each case mixes short conversational turns with long-context document tasks, orders short and long requests in a beneficiary-rich sequence, and assigns Poisson arrival times so that overlap and queueing are created by the workload rather than by a synthetic all-at-once burst.
why_real: UltraChat supplies short interactive prompts that resemble assistant turns and follow-up questions.
LongBench supplies long-context prompts such as report summarization and long-document QA.
Poisson arrivals provide a standard online-serving approximation for independent user arrivals with bursty overlap.
Keeping request counts fixed while sweeping arrival rate isolates traffic density as the main controlled stress variable.
Using locally cached model snapshots and local workload generation keeps the experiment reproducible on this machine without hardcoded model paths in code.

models.label: Gemma-7B-IT
models.reason: Primary non-Mistral reference model with a strong prior Phase I+II signal on V1.

models.label: Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2
models.reason: Instruction-tuned sibling used to test whether serving gains survive alignment changes.

datasets.label: UltraChat200k
datasets.reason: Provides short and medium-length conversational prompts that represent assistant traffic.

datasets.label: LongBench
datasets.reason: Provides long prompts from summarization and long-document QA tasks that exercise head-of-line blocking.

densities.name: low
densities.reason: Requests still overlap, but sustained queueing is limited. This checks that the policy does not rely on artificial saturation to show gains.

densities.name: mid
densities.reason: This is the center operating band where short and long requests overlap frequently enough to expose meaningful tradeoffs without collapsing into full saturation.

densities.name: high
densities.reason: This density creates repeated head-of-line blocking and is the first regime where strong Phase I+II rescues should become clearly visible if the implementation is working.

densities.name: peak
densities.reason: This upper-band density keeps the queue pressure high enough to reveal robustness limits and wall-time regressions that would be hidden at lighter traffic.
